text:
CT CHEST / ABDOMEN / PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
RESTAGING EXAMINATION

CLINICAL INDICATION: 59-year-old woman with metastatic triple-negative breast carcinoma, known hepatic metastases (right hepatic lobe, segments VI and VII) and right posterior 8th rib lytic lesion, currently receiving sacituzumab govitecan plus ola. Restaging to assess treatment response.

COMPARISON: Prior CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (approximately 3 months prior). Prior CT rib series and bone scan (approximately 3 months prior).

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following administration of 100 mL of intravenous iohexol contrast material (Omnipaque 300). Images were acquired in the portal venous phase. Axial images were obtained at 2.5 mm slice thickness with multiplanar reformations in the coronal and sagittal planes. Standard lung windows, mediastinal windows, and bone windows were reviewed.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are well aerated bilaterally. No focal consolidation, mass, or suspicious pulmonary nodule is identified. The previously described areas of ground-glass opacity in the left lower lobe, corresponding to the prior radiation therapy field, have resolved completely, consistent with resolution of radiation pneumonitis on prior imaging. The central airways are patent. No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax.

Mediastinum and Hila: The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The aorta and great vessels are unremarkable in caliber. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal, hilar, or subcarinal lymph nodes are identified. The thyroid gland, where visualized, appears within normal limits.

Breast / Chest Wall: Post-surgical and post-radiation changes are noted along the left anterior chest wall, consistent with the known left modified radical mastectomy and post-mastectomy radiation therapy. Bilateral tissue expanders are in situ and demonstrate expected post-placement appearance. There is mild soft tissue thickening along the left chest wall at the prior radiation field, unchanged from prior imaging. No discrete chest wall mass or nodular soft tissue density to suggest local recurrence at this time. No pathologically enlarged left or right axillary lymph nodes.

Bones (Chest): Targeted review of the right posterior 8th rib at the site of the previously confirmed lytic metastasis demonstrates an approximately 1.5 cm lytic lesion, essentially stable in size and morphology compared to prior imaging. There is no interval expansion of the lytic defect, no associated soft tissue mass, and no pathologic cortical disruption to suggest impending fracture. No new focal lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions are identified within the thorax. Mild multilevel degenerative changes of the thoracic spine are unchanged.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size with smooth contour and homogeneous background parenchyma. There is no gross hepatic steatosis. In hepatic segment VI, a hypoattenuating lesion previously measuring 1.4 x 1.2 cm now measures approximately 0.7 x 0.6 cm in greatest axial dimensions — representing a marked reduction in size, exceeding 50% decrease in longest diameter compared to the prior examination. The margins of this residual focus are somewhat indistinct and irregular, raising the possibility of a mixture of residual viable tumor and treatment-related fibrotic/necrotic change. Enhancement characteristics are diminished relative to the prior study, with less avid arterial phase uptake and less prominent wash-out on portal venous phase compared to initial characterization. In hepatic segment VII, the previously noted 0.8 cm hypoattenuating lesion is no longer reliably identified as a discrete focus on the current examination; no corresponding enhancing nodule or residual mass is seen in this location, and the area appears indistinguishable from the surrounding parenchyma. This likely represents near-complete to complete resolution of the segment VII lesion, though residual microscopic disease cannot be excluded. No new focal hepatic lesions are identified. No biliary ductal dilatation. The hepatic veins and portal vasculature appear patent.

Gallbladder and Biliary System: The gallbladder is present and unremarkable. No gallstones, wall thickening, or pericholecystic fluid. The common bile duct measures 4 mm, within normal limits.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation at 11.5 cm craniocaudal dimension. No focal splenic lesion.

Pancreas: The pancreatic body and tail are well visualized and unremarkable. The pancreatic head is partially obscured by overlying bowel gas but appears grossly normal. No ductal dilatation. No peripancreatic stranding.

Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are thin and uniform in morphology. No adrenal mass or nodularity.

Kidneys and Ureters: Both kidneys are present and symmetrically enhance without focal cortical lesion, hydronephrosis, or hydroureter. Renal cortical thinning is mild and symmetric, consistent with the known history of hypertension-related nephropathy. No renal calculi are identified. No perinephric fluid collection.

Retroperitoneum and Mesentery: No pathologically enlarged retroperitoneal, para-aortic, or mesenteric lymph nodes. The aorta is normal in caliber. No abdominal aortic aneurysm. No free intraperitoneal fluid. No ascites.

Gastrointestinal Tract: The stomach, small bowel, and visualized colon appear within normal limits. No bowel obstruction or wall thickening. No pneumoperitoneum.

PELVIS:

Pelvic Organs: The uterus is present and appears atrophic, consistent with the patient's postmenopausal status. Both ovaries are not individually identified on this examination (not unusual in a postmenopausal patient). No pelvic mass or free fluid.

Lymph Nodes: No pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Bladder: The urinary bladder is partially filled with contrast material and appears wall-normal without focal mass or irregularity.

Bones (Abdomen/Pelvis): The lumbar spine and visualized pelvis demonstrate multilevel degenerative disc disease and facet arthrosis, unchanged from prior studies. No new lytic or sclerotic lesions are identified in the visualized axial skeleton or proximal femora.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. HEPATIC METASTASES — MARKED INTERVAL DECREASE IN SIZE, CONSISTENT WITH TREATMENT RESPONSE:
   - The previously identified right hepatic segment VI lesion has decreased substantially, from 1.4 cm to approximately 0.7 cm, representing more than a 50% reduction in the dominant dimension. Enhancement characteristics are diminished, suggesting partial devitalization of the lesion.
   - The right hepatic segment VII lesion, previously measuring 0.8 cm, is no longer identifiable as a discrete nodular focus and has effectively resolved on this examination.
   - No new hepatic lesions are identified.
   These findings indicate a meaningful radiographic treatment response in the liver to the ongoing systemic regimen.

2. RIGHT POSTERIOR 8TH RIB LYTIC LESION — STABLE:
   The previously confirmed lytic osseous metastasis in the right posterior 8th rib remains stable in size at approximately 1.5 cm without interval growth, new associated soft tissue component, or evidence of pathologic fracture.

3. NO NEW SITES OF METASTATIC DISEASE:
   No new pulmonary, hepatic, osseous, lymph nodal, or peritoneal lesions are identified to suggest interval metastatic progression elsewhere.

4. POST-TREATMENT CHANGES:
   Post-surgical changes in the left chest wall following modified radical mastectomy, bilateral tissue expanders in situ, and post-radiation fibrotic changes in the left chest wall and left lower lobe are noted, all without features to suggest local or regional recurrence.

5. STABLE CHRONIC FINDINGS:
   Mild bilateral renal cortical changes consistent with hypertensive nephropathy, multilevel spinal degenerative changes, and absence of ascites or pleural effusion, all stable.

Overall, the imaging findings are consistent with a favorable partial treatment response to current systemic therapy, most evident at the hepatic metastatic sites. The solitary right rib lytic lesion remains structurally stable. Clinical correlation with tumor markers and patient performance status is advised.
